paper_a.title: Advances and Open Problems in Federated Learning

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.04977, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

paper_a.abstract: Federated learning (FL) is a machine learning setting where many clients (e.g. mobile devices or whole organizations) collaboratively train a model under the orchestration of a central server (e.g. service provider), while keeping the training data decentralized. FL embodies the principles of focused data collection and minimization, and can mitigate many of the systemic privacy risks and costs resulting from traditional, centralized machine learning and data science approaches. Motivated by the explosive growth in FL research, this paper discusses recent advances and presents an extensive collection of open problems and challenges.

paper_b.title: Federated Learning with Personalization Layers

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.00818, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

paper_b.abstract: The emerging paradigm of federated learning strives to enable collaborative training of machine learning models on the network edge without centrally aggregating raw data and hence, improving data privacy. This sharply deviates from traditional machine learning and necessitates the design of algorithms robust to various sources of heterogeneity. Specifically, statistical heterogeneity of data across user devices can severely degrade the performance of standard federated averaging for traditional machine learning applications like personalization with deep learning. This paper pro-posesFedPer, a base + personalization layer approach for federated training of deep feedforward neural networks, which can combat the ill-effects of statistical heterogeneity. We demonstrate effectiveness ofFedPerfor non-identical data partitions ofCIFARdatasetsand on a personalized image aesthetics dataset from Flickr.

hypotheses.title: Adaptive Personalization Layers for Extreme Data Heterogeneity in Federated Learning
hypotheses.hypothesis: Combining the systematic understanding of severe data heterogeneity as a critical 'Open Problem' in Federated Learning (as discussed in Paper A) with the architectural flexibility of 'Personalization Layers' (as introduced in Paper B) can lead to the development of novel, adaptive personalization layer designs that dynamically adjust their complexity and learning mechanisms, thereby significantly enhancing model performance, convergence stability, and fairness across highly non-IID client distributions beyond current static personalization approaches.
hypotheses.method: 1. Analyze Paper A and comprehensive FL surveys to precisely characterize the manifestations and limitations of current solutions for severe data heterogeneity. 2. Design and implement novel adaptive personalization layer architectures that can dynamically modify their structure, regularization, or aggregation strategies based on real-time client data distributions or model performance during federated training. 3. Evaluate these adaptive layers on diverse and highly non-IID datasets, comparing their efficacy against standard FL, existing personalized FL methods, and non-adaptive personalization layers in terms of accuracy, convergence speed, and fairness metrics.
hypotheses.impact: This research would provide a more robust and adaptable framework for personalized federated learning, making it viable for real-world scenarios with extreme data heterogeneity. It directly addresses a major roadblock in FL deployment, improving model utility, generalization, and equity across heterogeneous client populations, thus broadening the applicability of FL in complex environments.

hypotheses.title: Quantifiable Privacy Guarantees for Personalized Federated Learning Layers
hypotheses.hypothesis: Explicitly combining the critical 'Open Problems' related to privacy, adversarial robustness, and model inversion risks in Federated Learning (as highlighted in Paper A) with the specific architectural components of 'Personalization Layers' (from Paper B) can enable the development of novel, provably private personalization mechanisms that offer quantifiable privacy guarantees for client-specific model components, mitigating identified privacy risks without excessively sacrificing the benefits of personalization or overall model utility.
hypotheses.method: 1. Review Paper A and privacy-focused FL literature to identify specific privacy attack vectors (e.g., membership inference, reconstruction attacks) that are unique to or exacerbated by personalized components in FL. 2. Propose and implement formal privacy mechanisms, such as differentially private training, aggregation, or secure multi-party computation, specifically tailored for the personalization layers (e.g., noise injection into personalized gradients, secure computation for personalized model updates). 3. Conduct empirical evaluations comparing the utility and privacy guarantees (using metrics like privacy budget, attack success rate, and model accuracy) of the proposed DP-personalized FL framework against non-DP personalized FL and standard DP-FL methods on relevant privacy-sensitive tasks.
hypotheses.impact: This research would significantly advance the trustworthiness and security of personalized federated learning, making it a more viable solution for privacy-sensitive applications. It provides practical methodologies for deploying personalized FL with strong, auditable privacy guarantees, thereby expanding its adoption in critical sectors where data confidentiality is paramount, bridging the gap between customization and privacy requirements.

paper_a.abstract: Federated learning (FL) is a machine learning setting where many clients (e.g. mobile devices or whole organizations) collaboratively train a model under the orchestration of a central server (e.g. service provider), while keeping the training data decentralized. FL embodies the principles of focused data collection and minimization, and can mitigate many of the systemic privacy risks and costs resulting from traditional, centralized machine learning and data science approaches. Motivated by the explosive growth in FL research, this paper discusses recent advances and presents an extensive collection of open problems and challenges.

paper_b.title: Clustered Federated Learning: Model-Agnostic Distributed Multitask Optimization Under Privacy Constraints

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/1910.01991, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

paper_b.abstract: Federated learning (FL) is currently the most widely adopted framework for collaborative training of (deep) machine learning models under privacy constraints. Albeit its popularity, it has been observed that FL yields suboptimal results if the local clients’ data distributions diverge. To address this issue, we present clustered FL (CFL), a novel federated multitask learning (FMTL) framework, which exploits geometric properties of the FL loss surface to group the client population into clusters with jointly trainable data distributions. In contrast to existing FMTL approaches, CFL does not require any modifications to the FL communication protocol to be made, is applicable to general nonconvex objectives (in particular, deep neural networks), does not require the number of clusters to be known a priori, and comes with strong mathematical guarantees on the clustering quality. CFL is flexible enough to handle client populations that vary over time and can be implemented in a privacy-preserving way. As clustering is only performed after FL has converged to a stationary point, CFL can be viewed as a postprocessing method that will always achieve greater or equal performance than conventional FL by allowing clients to arrive at more specialized models. We verify our theoretical analysis in experiments with deep convolutional and recurrent neural networks on commonly used FL data sets.

hypotheses.title: Communication-Efficient Clustered Federated Learning
hypotheses.hypothesis: Integrating communication optimization techniques within a Clustered Federated Learning (CFL) framework can significantly reduce overall network bandwidth usage and latency without compromising model performance on heterogeneous data distributions.
hypotheses.method: Develop hierarchical aggregation schemes where clients first perform localized model averaging within their assigned clusters, sending only consolidated updates to the central server. Additionally, explore adaptive compression and sparsification strategies tailored specifically for inter-cluster communication.
hypotheses.impact: Improve the scalability and practicality of federated learning for large-scale deployments, especially in environments with limited bandwidth or high latency, by accelerating training convergence and minimizing network overhead, thereby addressing a key 'open problem' in FL.

hypotheses.title: Resource-Aware Clustered Federated Learning
hypotheses.hypothesis: Incorporating client resource heterogeneity (e.g., compute power, memory, battery life) into the clustering and model optimization processes of Federated Learning enables more efficient and equitable resource utilization across diverse client populations.
hypotheses.method: Design clustering algorithms that consider both data distribution similarities and client resource profiles. Implement adaptive training strategies within each cluster, such as assigning appropriate model complexities or varying local training epochs based on the aggregated available resources of cluster members.
hypotheses.impact: Enhance the inclusivity and robustness of FL by accommodating a wider range of client devices, minimizing client dropouts due to resource exhaustion, and optimizing overall training efficiency and sustainability, which is crucial for FL systems involving mobile devices.

paper_a.title: Federated Learning with Non-IID Data

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/1806.00582, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

paper_a.abstract: Federated learning enables resource-constrained edge compute devices, such as mobile phones and IoT devices, to learn a shared model for prediction, while keeping the training data local. This decentralized approach to train models provides privacy, security, regulatory and economic benefits. In this work, we focus on the statistical challenge of federated learning when local data is non-IID. We first show that the accuracy of federated learning reduces significantly, by up to 55% for neural networks trained for highly skewed non-IID data, where each client device trains only on a single class of data. We further show that this accuracy reduction can be explained by the weight divergence, which can be quantified by the earth mover's distance (EMD) between the distribution over classes on each device and the population distribution. As a solution, we propose a strategy to improve training on non-IID data by creating a small subset of data which is globally shared between all the edge devices. Experiments show that accuracy can be increased by 30% for the CIFAR-10 dataset with only 5% globally shared data.

paper_b.title: Membership Inference Attacks and Defenses in Federated Learning: A Survey

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.06157, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

paper_b.abstract: Federated learning is a decentralized machine learning approach where clients train models locally and share model updates to develop a global model. This enables low-resource devices to collaboratively build a high-quality model without requiring direct access to the raw training data. However, despite only sharing model updates, federated learning still faces several privacy vulnerabilities. One of the key threats is membership inference attacks, which target clients’ privacy by determining whether a specific example is part of the training set. These attacks can compromise sensitive information in real-world applications, such as medical diagnoses within a healthcare system. Although there has been extensive research on membership inference attacks, a comprehensive and up-to-date survey specifically focused on it within federated learning is still absent. To fill this gap, we categorize and summarize membership inference attacks and their corresponding defense strategies based on their characteristics in this setting. We introduce a unique taxonomy of existing attack research and provide a systematic overview of various countermeasures. For these studies, we thoroughly analyze the strengths and weaknesses of different approaches. Finally, we identify and discuss key future research directions for readers interested in advancing the field.

hypotheses.title: Impact of Non-IID Data on Membership Inference Attacks in Federated Learning
hypotheses.hypothesis: The presence of non-IID (non-independently and identically distributed) data across clients in a federated learning environment may significantly alter the effectiveness of membership inference attacks (MIAs). Specifically, highly unique or skewed data distributions on a client might either increase its vulnerability to MIAs due to more distinguishable patterns or, conversely, decrease vulnerability if the global model's generalization across diverse data makes specific member identification harder.
hypotheses.method: 1. Implement a federated learning framework with various non-IID data partitioning strategies (e.g., label skew, feature skew, quantity skew) on benchmark datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet). 2. Train global models under these non-IID conditions. 3. Apply state-of-the-art membership inference attacks (e.g., black-box attacks based on confidence scores or loss values) against the trained models. 4. Evaluate the MIA success rates (e.g., attack accuracy, AUC) across different non-IID settings and compare them to IID baselines to quantify the impact of data heterogeneity.
hypotheses.impact: This research would provide critical insights into the privacy implications of deploying federated learning in realistic non-IID data environments. Understanding how data heterogeneity influences MIA vulnerability is crucial for designing targeted and effective privacy-preserving mechanisms that are robust under real-world conditions, thereby fostering more secure FL deployments.

hypotheses.title: Adaptive Defense Mechanisms Against Membership Inference Attacks in Non-IID Federated Learning
hypotheses.hypothesis: Existing privacy-preserving defense mechanisms against membership inference attacks (MIAs), often designed or evaluated under IID assumptions, may exhibit reduced efficacy or introduce undue utility loss when applied to federated learning systems with non-IID data. Developing adaptive defenses that dynamically adjust their privacy budget or strategy based on the detected degree and type of non-IIDness can provide a superior trade-off between privacy protection and model utility.
hypotheses.method: 1. Set up a federated learning environment with varying degrees and types of non-IID data distribution. 2. Implement and evaluate the performance of several existing MIA defenses (e.g., differential privacy, knowledge distillation, confidence regularization) under these non-IID conditions, measuring both MIA success rate and global model accuracy. 3. Propose and develop novel or adapted defense mechanisms that explicitly account for non-IID data characteristics (e.g., client-adaptive differential privacy budgets, aggregation strategies sensitive to data skew, personalized regularization). 4. Compare the privacy-utility trade-offs of these adaptive defenses against existing methods in non-IID FL scenarios.
hypotheses.impact: This work would lead to the development of more robust and practical privacy-preserving federated learning systems. By tailoring defenses to the complexities of non-IID data, it can significantly enhance the protection against membership inference attacks without severely compromising the collaborative model's performance, thereby accelerating the adoption of FL in privacy-sensitive and heterogeneous data settings like healthcare and IoT.

paper_a.title: How To Backdoor Federated Learning

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/1807.00459, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

paper_a.abstract:
Federated learning enables thousands of participants to construct a deep learning model without sharing their private training data with each other. For example, multiple smartphones can jointly train a next-word predictor for keyboards without revealing what individual users type. We demonstrate that any participant in federated learning can introduce hidden backdoor functionality into the joint global model, e.g., to ensure that an image classifier assigns an attacker-chosen label to images with certain features, or that a word predictor completes certain sentences with an attacker-chosen word. 
We design and evaluate a new model-poisoning methodology based on model replacement. An attacker selected in a single round of federated learning can cause the global model to immediately reach 100% accuracy on the backdoor task. We evaluate the attack under different assumptions for the standard federated-learning tasks and show that it greatly outperforms data poisoning. Our generic constrain-and-scale technique also evades anomaly detection-based defenses by incorporating the evasion into the attacker's loss function during training.

paper_b.title: A survey on federated learning: challenges and applications

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650178, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

paper_b.abstract: Federated learning (FL) is a secure distributed machine learning paradigm that addresses the issue of data silos in building a joint model. Its unique distributed training mode and the advantages of security aggregation mechanism are very suitable for various practical applications with strict privacy requirements. However, with the deployment of FL mode into practical application, some bottlenecks appear in the FL training process, which affects the performance and efficiency of the FL model in practical applications. Therefore, more researchers have paid attention to the challenges of FL and sought for various effective research methods to solve these current bottlenecks. And various research achievements of FL have been made to promote the intelligent development of all application areas with privacy restriction. This paper systematically introduces the current researches in FL from five aspects: the basics knowledge of FL, privacy and security protection mechanisms in FL, communication overhead challenges and heterogeneity problems of FL. Furthermore, we make a comprehensive summary of the research in practical applications and prospect the future research directions of FL.

hypotheses.title: A Specialized Survey on Backdoor Attacks in Federated Learning: Taxonomy, Countermeasures, and Future Directions
hypotheses.hypothesis: Combining the comprehensive survey methodology for Federated Learning (as exemplified by Paper B) with the specific focus on backdoor attack mechanisms and vulnerabilities (as detailed in Paper A) will yield a specialized survey paper that provides a structured taxonomy of backdooring techniques, a systematic review of current defense mechanisms, and identifies critical open challenges for secure FL development.
hypotheses.method: Conduct a systematic literature review to identify all known backdoor attack strategies in FL, categorizing them by their attack surface, adversary capabilities, and impact. Simultaneously, identify and classify proposed mitigation techniques. Structure these findings into a survey framework akin to Paper B, but with a singular, in-depth focus on backdooring, to present a holistic view of the threat landscape and research gaps.
hypotheses.impact: This specialized survey would serve as a definitive reference for researchers and practitioners, offering a deep, organized understanding of backdooring threats in FL. It would accelerate research into robust defenses by clearly delineating attack types, evaluating the effectiveness of existing countermeasures, and highlighting the most promising future research directions in FL security.

hypotheses.title: Assessing the Vulnerability of Federated Learning Applications to Backdoor Attacks: A Cross-Domain Survey
hypotheses.hypothesis: By integrating a comprehensive understanding of various Federated Learning applications and their unique characteristics (as covered in surveys like Paper B) with a detailed analysis of backdoor attack methodologies (as explored in Paper A), it will be possible to create a novel survey that systematically assesses the vulnerability of different FL application domains to specific backdoor threats and proposes application-tailored security guidelines.
hypotheses.method: For various FL application domains (e.g., healthcare, IoT, finance, next-word prediction) typically discussed in general FL surveys, analyze how different backdoor attack vectors identified in Paper A could manifest and impact model utility or privacy within those specific contexts. Develop a comparative framework to rank applications based on their susceptibility to backdooring and formulate domain-specific security best practices.
hypotheses.impact: This combination would bridge the gap between theoretical attack research and practical application security. It would empower FL application developers with actionable insights into potential backdooring risks pertinent to their specific use cases, fostering the development of more resilient and trustworthy FL systems across diverse industries and enhancing the adoption of FL in sensitive domains.

paper_a.title: Federated Learning: Challenges, Methods, and Future Directions

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/1908.07873, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

paper_a.abstract: Federated learning involves training statistical models over remote devices or siloed data centers, such as mobile phones or hospitals, while keeping data localized. Training in heterogeneous and potentially massive networks introduces novel challenges that require a fundamental departure from standard approaches for large-scale machine learning, distributed optimization, and privacy-preserving data analysis. In this article, we discuss the unique characteristics and challenges of federated learning, provide a broad overview of current approaches, and outline several directions of future work that are relevant to a wide range of research communities.

paper_b.title: Membership Inference Attacks and Defenses in Federated Learning: A Survey

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.06157, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

paper_b.abstract: Federated learning is a decentralized machine learning approach where clients train models locally and share model updates to develop a global model. This enables low-resource devices to collaboratively build a high-quality model without requiring direct access to the raw training data. However, despite only sharing model updates, federated learning still faces several privacy vulnerabilities. One of the key threats is membership inference attacks, which target clients’ privacy by determining whether a specific example is part of the training set. These attacks can compromise sensitive information in real-world applications, such as medical diagnoses within a healthcare system. Although there has been extensive research on membership inference attacks, a comprehensive and up-to-date survey specifically focused on it within federated learning is still absent. To fill this gap, we categorize and summarize membership inference attacks and their corresponding defense strategies based on their characteristics in this setting. We introduce a unique taxonomy of existing attack research and provide a systematic overview of various countermeasures. For these studies, we thoroughly analyze the strengths and weaknesses of different approaches. Finally, we identify and discuss key future research directions for readers interested in advancing the field.

hypotheses.title: Impact of Federated Heterogeneity on Membership Inference Attack Vulnerability
hypotheses.hypothesis: Combining an understanding of Federated Learning's inherent heterogeneity (such as varying data distributions, device capabilities, and network conditions described in Paper A) with the study of Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) and defenses (Paper B) reveals that heterogeneity significantly modulates both the success rate of MIAs and the efficacy of existing defense mechanisms, potentially creating new attack vectors or rendering current defenses less effective in diverse FL environments.
hypotheses.method: Design and execute federated learning experiments with controlled variability in client data distributions, computational resources, and communication patterns. Apply a suite of advanced MIA techniques to these heterogeneous FL setups and quantify attack success rates. Implement and evaluate state-of-the-art MIA defenses under these diverse heterogeneity profiles to assess their robustness and identify specific vulnerabilities or improvements introduced by heterogeneity. Statistical analysis will be used to correlate heterogeneity metrics with MIA performance and defense effectiveness.
hypotheses.impact: This research would provide crucial insights for designing more robust and privacy-aware federated learning systems by identifying specific aspects of FL heterogeneity that exacerbate or mitigate MIA risks. It would guide the development of adaptive and context-aware defense strategies tailored to the complex realities of real-world federated deployments, improving privacy guarantees without unduly sacrificing model utility.

hypotheses.title: Designing Intrinsically MIA-Resistant Federated Learning Architectures for Future Directions
hypotheses.hypothesis: By integrating privacy-by-design principles directly into novel Federated Learning architectures and training methodologies (e.g., advanced client selection, secure aggregation protocols, or adaptive model update strategies, drawing from Paper A's 'methods and future directions') rather than solely relying on post-hoc defenses, it is possible to develop FL systems that are intrinsically more robust against Membership Inference Attacks (Paper B) while maintaining competitive model utility and addressing other FL challenges.
hypotheses.method: Propose and develop novel federated learning architectures and training protocols that embed privacy-enhancing mechanisms directly into their core design, leveraging insights from general FL challenges, methods, and future directions. Implement these new architectures and conduct comprehensive comparisons against baseline FL systems equipped with state-of-the-art MIA defenses (e.g., DP-SGD, knowledge distillation-based defenses). Evaluate MIA resistance using diverse attack strategies and assess the privacy-utility trade-offs, convergence speed, and communication efficiency of the proposed systems.
hypotheses.impact: This work would shift the paradigm from reactive MIA defense to proactive privacy-preserving FL design, leading to the creation of more fundamentally secure and trustworthy federated systems. By offering enhanced privacy guarantees built into the architecture itself, it would foster wider adoption of federated learning in highly sensitive domains such as healthcare, finance, and critical infrastructure, accelerating its real-world impact and addressing a key security challenge from a foundational perspective.
